title: FileIndex score_match uses wrapping_sub(1) on usize creating obscure underflow path
description:
## Severity
Minor

## Dimension
Cognitive Readability / KISS

## Location
src/tools/file_index.rs:152-158 (CamelCase bonus check inside score_match)

## Violation
```rust
if orig_chars[pi].is_uppercase()
    && orig_chars
        .get(pi.wrapping_sub(1))
        .is_some_and(|c| c.is_lowercase())
{
    score += BONUS_CAMEL;
}
```

The code is inside a branch guarded by `if pi > 0` (line 146) — so `pi.wrapping_sub(1)` will never actually underflow. The wrapping_sub is therefore a dead defensive measure that obscures intent: a reader has to mentally execute the path to confirm safety. KISS §3.2 demands the obvious form: `orig_chars.get(pi - 1)`. The wrapping arithmetic is a "clever" pattern that suggests the programmer was uncertain about the invariant.

Worse, the cast at line 184 uses `#[allow(clippy::cast_possible_truncation, clippy::cast_possible_wrap)]` — silencing the lint instead of bounding `path_chars.len()` first. The path_len_penalty division yields zero for very long paths, defeating the "Prefer shorter paths" intent for the worst offenders.

## Mandated refactor
1. Replace `pi.wrapping_sub(1)` with `pi - 1`. The `if pi > 0` guard already enforces safety.
2. Replace the `#[allow]` casts with `i32::try_from(path_chars.len()).unwrap_or(i32::MAX) / 10` — the value cannot legitimately overflow because paths are bounded by OS PATH_MAX.

## Filed from QA review dispatch
